headlines.title: JAP LEADER LAUDS U. S.
headlines.subtitle: Baron Kato Is Pleased With Stand in War Taken by United States. PEVIEWS THE CONFLICT Minister Says German Base at Kiao Chow Menaced Entire Orient.
headlines.fullText:
TOKIO, Sept. 5. — By Associated Press. — Amgaron Kato, minister of foreign affairs, reviewed at the opening of the diet today the events leading up to the war with Germany. He said that England asked Japan's assistance and that Japan had been glad to give it. After a full and frank exchange of views, Japan's ultimatum, which was the same as that sent by England, was dispatched.
Kato closed his speech with a tribute to the United States government.
"To the American government for the courtesy it has been good enough to extend to Japan in connection with the present international trouble," he said, "and for the protection of Japan's subjects and interests in Germany and Austria, I desire to express the sincere appreciation of the Imperial government."
The statement to the diet made by Jaren Kato, minister of foreign affairs, follows:
Kato's Statement.
"Early in August the British government asked the Imperial government for assistance under the terms of the Anglo-Japanese alliance. German warships were prowling the seas of Eastern Asia, a menace to our commerce and that of our ally, while Kiao Chow was being utilized for the purpose of constituting a base for warlike operations in the Far East. No doubt was thus left as to the purpose of Germany.
"The alliance between Japan and Great Britain has for its object the maintenance of general peace in Eastern Asia.
German base a menace.
"Germany's possession of a base for warlike activities in one corner of the Far East was not only a serious obstacle to the maintenance of permanent peace, but it also conflicted with the immediate interests of the Japanese empire. The Japanese government, therefore, resolved to comply with the British request, and if necessary, to open hostilities against Germany. After Imperial sanction had been obtained, this position was communicated to the British government and a full and frank exchange of views followed between them for the purpose of defining their general interests. Japan has no desire to become involved in the present conflict."

headlines.title: SCARCITY OF BRITISH WAR NEWS SCORED
headlines.subtitle: London Times Criticizes Authorities—Points to German Example.
headlines.fullText:
LONDON, Sept. 5. — By Associated Press. — The Times, speaking editorially of the lack of official war news, says:
"Premier Asquith said on Monday that the government felt that the public was entitled to prompt authentic information of what happened at the front and that means were being taken for making arrangements. We wish we could see any sign that these arrangements have been made. The war has been in progress for a month now and even during that period the public has been furnished with only one adequate official statement regarding the land operations of the most powerful army Great Britain ever sent to the continent.
"In time of an unprecedented crisis the press has a great patriotic duty to perform. It has to estimate and sharpen the public interest in this struggle in which is bound to last a very long time and on which the fate of the empire depends.
"Germany is well aware of the potent influence of the press and uses it to the uttermost.
"We commend to attention the facsimile we published today of a communication from a member of the Times' staff. This communication has been published in hundreds of newspapers in Germany and Austria. We can quote innumerable instances of the kind. One reason why Turkey is on the verge of war is that the capital is swamped by Germany with fabrications about alleged British and French reverses. Another example we quote shows that the German and Austrian press is now being mobilized for efforts to detach France from the entente.
"Germany has even approached the Times with a request to publish a statement from her general staff upon the military operations.
"Our government has done nothing to counteract these statements of any value. We have received from official sources less information than from our own war office."

headlines.title: Germany's World Trade Becomes Big War Prize
headlines.subtitle: British Especially Are Trying to Nose Kaiser Out of Commerce—Extent Is Surprising.
headlines.fullText:
Special to The Star-Telegram.
Canada's share of Germany's trade with the world is one of the prizes of war which England is trying to capture. While Great Britain's army is fighting the Prussian military advance in Belgium and the British fleet is opposing the Kaiser's warships in the North Sea, and with Germany's commerce swept from the oceans, those remaining behind in England are initiating a movement to capture the German and Austrian trade by invading the world markets to provide such products as could be manufactured in the United Kingdom.
Big Cutlery Trade.
One characteristic jolt was a discovery that Germany exported so much cutlery, a line of trade which heretofore had been held up as one in which England was pre-eminent. The men who are now entertaining the idea that Great Britain's loss of trade in Central Europe due to the war can more than be made up by capturing world trade heretofore held by Germany. They are even discussing the possibility of England taking over the German trade with America, amounting to $170,000,000 annually.
Another thing that is being emphasized is the German toy trade, on which it has had a monopoly. This is a trade which England certainly will invade. It runs into many millions. England alone imports millions of dollars worth of German toys.
British Government Aids.
The British movement to capture Germany's trade is being taken up with great vigor and the British government is giving its full official support. The Board of Trade, which in England is a government department, is doing everything possible. The consular office is gathering all available information from the dominions as to the character of German and Austrian imports. The Chamber of Commerce, at London, is acting as a go-between for the manufacturers and exporters to capture German products.
It is also shown conclusively that England can easily supersede Germany in many products. It is obvious that German commerce will be paralyzed for many months, possibly for years, during which time the far reaching requirements of the world, hitherto largely filled by Germany and Austria, must still be met. If the world's wants heretofore filled by Germany and Austria are not met by others, England is determined to grab a big share. The situation is summed up thus: that war is causing a loss to the English in the most likely places are those where Germany and Austria formerly predominated.
When England, shortly after the start of the war, began to investigate the situation, figures were produced which astounded the public. It was shown that Germany's exports were nearly $2,500,000,000 annually, which was rather disconcerting to those who had been taught to believe complacently that England was really the greatest manufacturing country in the world.

headlines.title: Alsace-Lorraine Caused War Lasting 1,000 Years
headlines.subtitle: Conflicts Began in 843 and Apparently Will Never End. Started by Charlemagne's Sons.
headlines.fullText:
History is but repeating itself once more in the big conflict where the French and Germans are battling in Alsace and Lorraine, the prize of war for more than a thousand years.
What is now Germany and France have fought one another with astonishing regularity for this territory, changing from one flag to another.
The history of France and Germany, as separate nations, begins in 843, and the tribulations of Alsace and Lorraine as the bone of contention between the two and as conquered first by the one and then by the other, began soon after.
Thousand Years of War.
The present campaign that France is waging in Alsace is therefore only the continuation of a struggle over 1,000 years old. The facts of geographical position have created the former. As long as the Rhine flows and the Vosges mountains stand, France will say that the Rhine is her true eastern frontier. Germany says that the frontier lies besides the mountains.
It was in 843 that the three grandsons of Charlemagne, fighting among themselves, decided to end the controversy by dividing their grandfather's possessions among themselves. They acted on the ancient principle that the lands and the people dwelling upon them, tilling the soil in peace and fighting the battles in war, were the private property of the sovereign. Charlemagne had ruled the whole of Europe as one united government with the exception alone of the farben. His son, Louis le Debonair, was too weak to hold together this heterogeneous empire of peoples of different race and temperament, and speaking different tongues, their only bond being an official religion, and their sovereign. He was too weak even to rule his own family and long before his death, his three sons were quarreling over their respective shares.
Sons Divide Empire.
These two brothers took land which formed a compact whole and could easily be defended. The people of Louis, now the Germans, spoke one language and the people of Charles, now the French, spoke another.
The third brother, Lothair, the weakest, had to take what was left of his father's empire. It included what is now Holland, Belgium, Luxemberg, Alsace, Lorraine and a strip of Northern Italy. It was peopled by many different nationalities. It was not easily defended because the Alps broke it into two parts, and the narrow strip along the Rhine from the Alps to the sea was too great a prize not to be coveted by the descendants of Louis from touching those of Charles. Soon they began to fight one another about it, each to take it away from Lothair.
Thus the troubles of the buffer states began.
Swiss Gain Freedom.
The mountaineers of Switzerland won their freedom in the Thirteenth century and have been a republic ever since. The people of Holland gained their independence under the leadership of William of Orange in 1548, but the successors of Charles the Bold, ruling France, conquered them later and it was not until 1815 that they were free again. Belgium was separated from Holland in 1830.
Luxemburg was promised to France by Prussia if France would stay out of the war. When the war was over, the descendants of Louis the German refused to give it to the descendants of Charles the Bold. Luxemburg accordingly was made independent and the guns of her forts taken away that she might not fight on either side. The small strip of Northern Italy which belonged to the kingdom of Lothair was, after many vicissitudes, incorporated in the union of Italian states under Victor Emmanuel II.
Alsace and Lorraine have alone remained of the empire of Lothair to be fought over by the two great nations on either side. Together they cover an area of 5,601 miles.
During the Franco-Prussian war of 1870 when Germany was the victor, almost all the great battles were fought in Alsace and Lorraine. At the commencement of the present war, France immediately invaded Alsace to wipe out the disgrace of 1870.

headlines.title: BOARD DELAYS HEMPHILL ST. PAVING ACTION
headlines.subtitle: City Commission Holds Twenty-Nine Minute Session. $25,000 Claim Filed.
headlines.fullText:
The city commission held a short week-end session Saturday. Just twenty-nine minutes were required to approve bills and receive communications, which were filed with the heads of various departments.
Owing to the absence of Commissioner Grant, the expected action on the paving work on Hemphill Street was not taken. An ordinance, also drawn by McCart, which is intended to obviate future embarrassments arising from similar situations. McCart holds that the city entered into a contract for paving, despite the fact that the law requires property being connected with water and sewer mains before the street in question is paved. The stretch of Hemphill Street has no connection with the sewer main.
The ordinance, which will probably be introduced at the next commission meeting, will regulate the letting of contracts for paving. It would require the city engineer to make a report of the condition of streets as to water and sewer mains and give an estimate as to the cost of the laying of the mains. It forbids the commission to order the paving of any street or alley until the water and sewer mains are laid.
A petition, signed by property owners asking that Twenty-first street, be graded and graveled, is also awaiting action.
Mrs. Willie State filed a claim with the commission for $25,000, alleging that her husband, formerly a city fireman, was injured by being thrown from a fire engine while in the discharge of his duty and has been made unsafe.
Land Agent Davis reported that the city had been offered a tract of land near Lake Worth at $50 per acre. It is to be purchased from William Fund.
Building Inspector Allen reported that seepage from the flower beds about city hall is menacing the outer walls of the building and will in time make them unsafe unless waterproof concrete pits are constructed.
John Dobbins and Fred Southers were named for special police service.

headlines.title: RUSSIANS GET VAST AUSTRIAN ARMY STORES
headlines.fullText:
LONDON, Sept. 5. — A dispatch to the Reuter Telegram company from Petrograd, describing the Russian advance on Lemberg, says:
"The Austrians are concentrating two more army corps toward the east of their position to face the Russian Kief district army, but the Russians attacked before the concentration was completed.
"On Wednesday morning the Russians were established all around the northern, eastern and southern suburbs in face of the capital. Lemberg stands high above the surrounding country, its war defenses being supplemented by the nature of the ground. It would seem that the rout of the Austrian army, whose double duty was to cover Lemberg and also the left flank of the Austrian center, must have been complete, for the Russians must have entered Lemberg at the heels of the runaways, for the entire military stores of every kind, explosives, powder magazines, commissariat, fell intact in the hands of the victors.
"In towns in Russia with a German name were changed to the Slav form."

headlines.title: BORDEAUX CONFIRMS GERMANS DIVERTED
headlines.fullText:
LONDON, Sept. 5. — The first great climax of the campaign of the allies against Germany waged so recklessly for weeks past in Northern France, will not now be long delayed, according to opinion in London.
The first official communique from Bordeaux, the new seat of the French government, speaks of the German movement as being diverted to the eastward, in which direction the invaders have reached La Ferte-sous-Jouarre, some thirty-five miles east of Paris. This would seem to confirm the German claim that the allied forces have been driven back to and in some cases, across the Marne river. According to other sources of information, the Germans are much nearer Paris than that, being within a few miles of Chantilly, twenty miles north of the capital.

headlines.title: POPE BENEDICT TO MAKE PEACE EFFORT
headlines.subtitle: Rome Report Announces Him as Holding Belief That Active Steps Should Be Taken.
headlines.fullText:
ROME, Sept. 4, via Paris, Sept. 5. — Before his election, Pope Benedict XV repeatedly expressed his belief in the necessity that the pontiff should intervene with an appeal for peace, not in a purely evangelical form, but in precise action.
"The pope," he is quoted as saying, "should not stand aloof from the combatants, but instead of keeping away and preaching peace and concord, he should intervene."
It is said he expressed these ideas with great tenacity, and that it brought about his election as pontiff.

headlines.title: CITY HEALTH REPORT GIVEN COMMISSION
headlines.subtitle: Ninety-Six Births and Seventy-Three Deaths During August—Not Sure All Former Reported.
headlines.fullText:
Fort Worth's population was increased by 96 new arrivals in the world during August, according to a report from Health Officer Hays. The total number of deaths was 73. Of that number, however, only nine were residents of Fort Worth.
"We are sure we got all of the deaths, but not so sure about the births," said Dr. Hays. "For instance, you will notice that only two negro births were reported."
Of the white babies born, 52 were boys and 44 girls.
The mortuary report shows that 60 white people and 13 negroes died. There were reported, together with 12 cases of diphtheria and five cases of scarlet fever. Fifty houses were reported fumigated.

headlines.title: Japan Cotton Goods Cheaper.
headlines.fullText: WASHINGTON, Sept. 5. — Successful competition against American cotton products in China is based largely on the availability of cheap labor and the low cost of raw material, the department of commerce announced today in detailing the results of a recent investigation. The cotton industry in China was developing. There were indications the Japanese spinners feared competition from the Chinese mills more than from American and Indian mills.

headlines.title: Lemberg's Fall Admitted.
headlines.fullText: LONDON, Sept. 5. — A dispatch to the Exchange from Rome says it is officially admitted in Vienna that the Russians have captured both Lemberg and Halicz.

headlines.title: GERMAN GENERAL STAFF MOVES TO MONS
headlines.fullText: LONDON, Sept. 5. — An Amsterdam dispatch to the Central News says the German general staff has moved from Brussels to Mons.

headlines.title: Machine Guns at Butte.
headlines.fullText: BUTTE, Mont., Sept. 5. — Militiamen maintaining peace in Butte were reinforced today by the arrival from Helena of four machine guns, making a detail of fifty men now here. All of the companies of the National Guard except those on leave of absence are now in camp.

headlines.title: 33,050 Canadians in Camp.
headlines.fullText: VALCARTIER, Quebec, Sept. 5. — The number of the troops from which will be selected the first Canadian contingent for the war is now 33,650.

headlines.title: Finance Plan Submitted.
headlines.fullText: Plans for meeting the foreign exchange situation and caring for American obligations to Europe were submitted today to the Federal reserve board by a committee of which James B. Forgan of Chicago, chairman of the bankers' committee, is a member. The report will be taken up Tuesday. There is no positive information as to the full amount, but it has been approximated at $150,000,000.

headlines.title: Dutch Will Go Home.
headlines.fullText: NEW YORK, Sept. 5. — Arrangements to send Dutch reservists back to The Netherlands to preserve the neutrality of that country were made today by Count de Beaufort, Netherlands minister to the United States.

headlines.title: Cubans Fight Sabre Duel.
headlines.fullText: HAVANA, Sept. 5. — Col. Orestes Ferrara, liberal leader in the house of representatives, and Maj. Armando Andre, a conservative member of the house and editor of the newspaper El Dia, fought a duel with sabres yesterday. Both combatants were severely wounded.
